meta.title: DBX-SDA-3: Testing, Debugging and Performance Tuning

questions.stem: In the Spark execution model, what defines the boundary between two **stages**?

options.text: Each transformation creates a new stage.
options.explanation: Narrow transformations (filter, select, map) do not create stage boundaries; they are pipelined within the same stage.

options.text: Each action creates a new stage.
options.explanation: Actions trigger job execution but do not themselves define stage boundaries within the DAG; stage boundaries are determined by the shuffle structure of transformations.

options.text: A wide transformation (shuffle) creates a stage boundary because data must be exchanged between partitions across executors.
options.explanation: Stage boundaries in Spark occur at shuffle points — when a wide transformation (groupBy, join, distinct, repartition) requires data to be redistributed across partitions. Spark cannot pipeline across a shuffle because data from all upstream partitions must be written before downstream tasks can start reading.

options.text: Stage boundaries are defined by the number of partitions: each partition is its own stage.
options.explanation: Partitions are the unit of parallelism within a stage (each task processes one partition); they do not define stage boundaries.

questions.stem: You call `df.cache()` and then immediately `df.count()`. Later in the same script you call `df.count()` again. How many times is the full computation (including source data reads) executed?

options.text: 0 times — `cache()` pre-loads the data into memory immediately.
options.explanation: `cache()` is a lazy operation; it does not read data immediately.

options.text: 2 times — the first `count()` computes and caches; the second `count()` invalidates the cache and recomputes.
options.explanation: Running an action on a cached DataFrame does not invalidate the cache; `unpersist()` is needed to explicitly remove it.

options.text: 2 times — each `count()` triggers a full recomputation because `cache()` only stores results temporarily.
options.explanation: Once cached successfully, subsequent actions on the same DataFrame reuse the cache — there is no recomputation.

options.text: 1 time — the first `count()` triggers computation and populates the cache; the second `count()` reads from cache.
options.explanation: `cache()` marks the DataFrame for caching but does not execute immediately — Spark is lazy. The first `count()` action triggers computation of the entire DAG (reads source, applies transformations) and stores the result in memory. The second `count()` reads from the in-memory cache without re-running the source computation.

questions.stem: Select TWO TRUE statements about the Spark **shuffle** operation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Shuffle involves writing intermediate data to disk and transferring it over the network between executors.
options.explanation: Shuffle involves writing shuffle map output files to local disk on each executor, then transferring the relevant partitions to reducer executors over the network. This disk I/O and network transfer is what makes shuffles expensive.

options.text: Shuffle is always avoided by Spark whenever a narrow transformation can be used instead.
options.explanation: While Spark does try to avoid unnecessary shuffles (e.g., skipping a sort if data is already sorted), operations like groupBy and join semantically require shuffles and cannot be replaced by narrow alternatives without fundamentally changing the computation.

options.text: Increasing `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` always improves performance by creating more parallelism.
options.explanation: Increasing shuffle partitions beyond the available parallelism (executors × cores) can hurt performance by creating overhead from many small tasks; the right value depends on data size.

options.text: Shuffle creates a stage boundary because all map-side output must be written before the reduce side can begin reading.
options.explanation: This is precisely why shuffles create stage boundaries — the reduce-side tasks cannot start until ALL map-side tasks have completed and written their output, breaking the pipeline.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of `df.explain()` in PySpark?

options.text: It executes the DataFrame and prints the first 20 rows.
options.explanation: `df.show()` displays rows; `explain()` shows the execution plan without processing data.

options.text: It prints the physical (and optionally logical) execution plan that Spark will use for the DataFrame, without executing it.
options.explanation: `df.explain()` prints the query execution plan(s) to the console. By default it shows the physical plan; with `extended=True` it also shows the logical plans. This is purely informational — no computation occurs.

options.text: It returns a Python string description of the DataFrame's schema.
options.explanation: `df.printSchema()` prints the schema in a tree format; `explain()` shows the query plan.

options.text: It launches the Spark UI so you can monitor the job.
options.explanation: The Spark UI is launched automatically when a SparkSession starts and runs as a background service; `explain()` does not interact with it.

questions.stem: A Spark executor has 8 GB of total memory. With the default `spark.memory.fraction` of 0.6 and `spark.memory.storageFraction` of 0.5, how much memory (approximately) is available for execution (shuffles, joins, sorts)?

options.text: 8 GB (all executor memory is available for execution)
options.explanation: Not all 8 GB is for execution; Spark reserves overhead for JVM and Python processes.

options.text: 4.8 GB (60% of 8 GB is the unified memory pool; half of that, 2.4 GB, is reserved for storage, leaving 2.4 GB for execution)
options.explanation: 4.8 GB is the full unified pool, not just execution memory.

options.text: 2.4 GB (execution starts with half of the unified pool, though storage/execution can borrow from each other)
options.explanation: With default settings, `spark.memory.fraction=0.6` means 60% of executor memory (minus 300 MB reserved) is the unified memory pool ≈ 4.8 GB. `spark.memory.storageFraction=0.5` means storage initially gets 50% of that pool (≈ 2.4 GB) and execution gets the other 50% (≈ 2.4 GB). Crucially, the unified memory model allows execution and storage to borrow from each other — if storage needs more, it can borrow from execution and vice versa. So initially ~2.4 GB is designated for execution.

options.text: 1.6 GB (only 20% of total memory is available for execution)
options.explanation: 1.6 GB corresponds to 20% of 8 GB, which doesn't match the described memory fractions.

questions.stem: Which code correctly selects the columns `name`, `age`, and a derived column `age_in_10_years` that is `age + 10` in a single `select()` call?

options.text: `df.select('name', 'age', col('age') + 10)`
options.explanation: `col('age') + 10` without `.alias()` produces the value, but the output column name is auto-generated rather than `age_in_10_years`.

options.text: `df.select('name', 'age', (col('age') + 10).alias('age_in_10_years'))`
options.explanation: `df.select('name', 'age', (col('age') + 10).alias('age_in_10_years'))` produces the required three-column result in a single `select()` call, and `.alias()` gives the derived expression the required output name.

options.text: `df.select('name', 'age').withColumn('age_in_10_years', col('age') + 10)`
options.explanation: is wrong for this question because it uses two transformations (`select()` and then `withColumn()`), even though it would still produce the requested columns. The stem asks specifically for a single `select()` call.

options.text: `df.select('name', 'age').addColumn('age_in_10_years', col('age') + 10)`
options.explanation: `addColumn()` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method. The correct methods for adding columns are `withColumn()` or including expressions in `select()`.

questions.stem: You want to filter a DataFrame to rows where `department` is one of 'Engineering', 'Data Science', or 'Analytics'. Which approach uses the most idiomatic PySpark method?

options.text: `df.filter((col('department') == 'Engineering') | (col('department') == 'Data Science') | (col('department') == 'Analytics'))`
options.explanation: Chaining multiple `|` operators produces correct results but is verbose and non-idiomatic compared to `isin()` when checking membership in a fixed set of values.

options.text: `df.filter(col('department').isin('Engineering', 'Data Science', 'Analytics'))`
options.explanation: `col('department').isin('Engineering', 'Data Science', 'Analytics')` uses `isin()` with varargs — this is the most idiomatic PySpark pattern shown in official documentation and community examples. Passing values as positional arguments is the conventional style.

options.text: `df.filter(col('department').isin(['Engineering', 'Data Science', 'Analytics']))`
options.explanation: While `isin()` technically accepts a Python list in some Spark versions, the documented signature is `isin(*cols)` using varargs. Passing a wrapped list as a single argument is a common mistake and the less conventional pattern — B with explicit varargs is the standard form.

options.text: `df.filter(col('department').in_(['Engineering', 'Data Science', 'Analytics']))`
options.explanation: `.in_()` is not a valid PySpark Column method. The correct method is `.isin()`.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame `df` with 1000 partitions after a shuffle. You need to write it to 10 Parquet files. Which code avoids a full shuffle while reducing the partition count?

options.text: `df.repartition(10).write.parquet('/output')`
options.explanation: `repartition(10)` performs a full shuffle to redistribute data evenly across 10 partitions — this is correct if you need uniform partition sizes, but the question specifically asks to avoid a full shuffle.

options.text: `df.write.option('maxPartitions', 10).parquet('/output')`
options.explanation: `write.option('maxPartitions', 10).parquet(...)` is not a valid write option for Parquet in PySpark. There is no `maxPartitions` option on the DataFrameWriter.

options.text: `df.repartition(10, col('id')).write.parquet('/output')`
options.explanation: `repartition(10, col('id'))` also performs a full shuffle (hash-partitioned on 'id'), which the question requires avoiding.

options.text: `df.coalesce(10).write.parquet('/output')`
options.explanation: `coalesce(10)` reduces the number of partitions from 1000 to 10 without performing a full shuffle. It works by merging existing partitions on the same executor, which is a narrow transformation. This is the preferred approach when you only need to reduce partitions and even distribution is less critical.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about inner joins in PySpark. (Select TWO)

options.text: An inner join returns only rows where the join key exists in BOTH DataFrames.
options.explanation: By definition, an inner join filters to rows that have matching keys in both DataFrames — unmatched rows from either side are excluded.

options.text: If both DataFrames have a column with the same name (other than the join key), PySpark raises an error unless you alias or drop the duplicate.
options.explanation: PySpark does NOT raise an error for duplicate column names after a join; it allows the join and produces a DataFrame with duplicate column names — however, selecting those ambiguous columns later will raise an AnalysisException.

options.text: When joining on a column name string (`df1.join(df2, 'id')`), the resulting DataFrame contains only one `id` column.
options.explanation: When you pass a column name as a string to `join()`, PySpark merges the join key into a single column in the result, giving you one `id` column.

options.text: When joining on a Column expression (`df1.join(df2, df1.id == df2.id)`), the resulting DataFrame contains only one `id` column.
options.explanation: When you use a Column expression (`df1.id == df2.id`), PySpark keeps BOTH `id` columns in the result (one from each side), resulting in two columns named `id` — this is a subtle but important difference from string-based join.

questions.stem: Which function computes the `lag` of a column (the value from the previous row) within a window partition?

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time')
df.withColumn('prev_value', lag('value', 1).over(w))
```
options.explanation: `lag(column, offset)` returns the value from `offset` rows before the current row within the window partition. With `offset=1`, it gives the immediately preceding row's value. `orderBy` is essential to define 'previous'.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lead
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time')
df.withColumn('prev_value', lead('value', 1).over(w))
```
options.explanation: `lead(column, offset)` returns the value from `offset` rows AFTER the current row (the next row), not the previous row.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import lag
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id')
df.withColumn('prev_value', lag('value', 1).over(w))
```
options.explanation: `lag()` requires an `orderBy` in the window specification to have a meaningful concept of 'previous row'; without `orderBy`, the order is undefined and Spark may raise an AnalysisException or return arbitrary results.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
from pyspark.sql.functions import shift
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time')
df.withColumn('prev_value', shift('value', -1).over(w))
```
options.explanation: `F.shift()` is not a function in pyspark.sql.functions; the correct functions are `lag()` and `lead()`.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with a nested StructType column. What is the correct way to access a nested field `address.city` where `address` is a StructType column containing a `city` string field?

options.text: `df.select(col('address.city'))`
options.explanation: Dot notation in the column string `col('address.city')` is the idiomatic way to navigate into a StructType column. Spark resolves `address.city` as 'the `city` field inside the `address` struct column', returning a Column of the nested field's type.

options.text: `df.select(col('address').getField('city'))`
options.explanation: While `col('address').getField('city')` is a valid method on a Column object and also navigates into the struct, it is a more verbose approach. The dot notation in A is the standard pattern taught in the Spark documentation.

options.text: `df.select(col('address')['city'])`
options.explanation: Subscript notation `col('address')['city']` is designed for MapType columns (string-keyed maps), not StructType. Using it on a struct column causes an AnalysisException.

options.text: `df.select(col('address').field('city'))`
options.explanation: `.field()` is not a valid PySpark Column method. There is no `field()` accessor — use dot notation (`col('address.city')`) or `.getField('city')` instead.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly reads a Delta table from a path?

options.text: `spark.read.table('/mnt/delta/table')`
options.explanation: `spark.read.table()` reads from the metastore catalog using a table name, not a file path; using a path string here would fail because it expects a registered table name like `'my_db.my_table'`, not a storage path.

options.text: `spark.read.delta('/mnt/delta/table')`
options.explanation: `spark.read.delta()` is not a standard shorthand method — unlike `spark.read.parquet()` or `spark.read.csv()`, there is no `.delta()` convenience method in the standard PySpark API.

options.text: `spark.read.format('parquet').load('/mnt/delta/table')`
options.explanation: Reading a Delta table with the Parquet format would read only the underlying Parquet data files, bypassing Delta's transaction log — you would get raw, potentially unmerged or uncommitted data.

options.text: `spark.read.format('delta').load('/mnt/delta/table')`
options.explanation: `spark.read.format('delta').load(path)` is the standard way to read a Delta table from a file path using the Delta Lake format.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about the difference between `rank()` and `dense_rank()` window functions. (Select TWO)

options.text: `rank()` assigns the same rank to tied rows but skips subsequent rank numbers (e.g., 1, 1, 3); `dense_rank()` assigns the same rank to tied rows but does not skip numbers (e.g., 1, 1, 2).
options.explanation: This is the textbook definition of `rank()` vs `dense_rank()`. With ties, `rank()` skips numbers to account for the number of tied rows, while `dense_rank()` uses consecutive integers.

options.text: `dense_rank()` always produces unique rank values with no ties; `rank()` allows ties.
options.explanation: `dense_rank()` also allows ties — tied rows get the same rank number; it just doesn't skip subsequent numbers.

options.text: If 3 rows are tied for rank 1, `rank()` assigns those rows ranks 1, 2, 3; `dense_rank()` assigns them all rank 1.
options.explanation: This describes `row_number()` behavior, which assigns unique sequential numbers even to ties (1, 2, 3 regardless of tied values). `rank()` with 3 ties would give 1, 1, 1.

options.text: If 3 rows are tied for rank 1, `rank()` assigns all three rows rank 1 and the next row gets rank 4; `dense_rank()` assigns all three rows rank 1 and the next row gets rank 2.
options.explanation: This describes the concrete behavior with 3 tied rows — `rank()` gives 1,1,1 then skips to 4 for the next row (because positions 1, 2, 3 are consumed by the ties); `dense_rank()` gives 1,1,1 then 2 for the next distinct value.

questions.stem: You want to replace all null values in a column `score` with 0 and all null values in `name` with 'Unknown' in a single operation. Which code is correct?

options.text: `df.fillna(0).fillna('Unknown')`
options.explanation: Chaining `df.fillna(0).fillna('Unknown')` applies each fill to ALL columns, not specific ones. The first call attempts to replace nulls in ALL columns with 0 (silently ignored for string columns), then the second replaces remaining nulls with 'Unknown'. This is not targeted and may affect unintended columns.

options.text: `df.replace({'score': 0, 'name': 'Unknown'})`
options.explanation: `df.replace()` is used to substitute EXISTING non-null values with other values (like replacing 'N/A' strings with 'Unknown'). It is not designed for null replacement — `replace()` with a dict replaces actual data values, not nulls.

options.text: `df.na.fill({'score': 0, 'name': 'Unknown'})`
options.explanation: `df.na.fill({'score': 0, 'name': 'Unknown'})` replaces null values in specific named columns using a dictionary. `df.na` accesses the `DataFrameNaFunctions` object, and `.fill(dict)` applies each column-to-value mapping in a single operation — the idiomatic approach.

options.text: `df.fillna(0, subset=['score']).fillna('Unknown', subset=['name'])`
options.explanation: While chaining `fillna(0, subset=['score'])` and `fillna('Unknown', subset=['name'])` correctly targets specific columns, it requires two transformation steps and creates an intermediate DataFrame. The single dict-based call in C is more concise and efficient.

questions.stem: You want to define an explicit schema for a CSV file to improve read performance. Which code correctly defines a schema with columns `id` (integer), `name` (string), and `score` (double)?

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, IntegerType, StringType, DoubleType
schema = StructType([
  StructField('id', IntegerType(), True),
  StructField('name', StringType(), True),
  StructField('score', DoubleType(), True)
])
spark.read.schema(schema).csv('/path/to/file.csv')
```
options.explanation: `StructType([StructField(...)])` from `pyspark.sql.types` is the canonical programmatic way to define an explicit schema. Each `StructField` specifies the column name, data type object, and nullable flag. This schema is passed via `.schema(schema)` on the reader.

options.text:
```python
schema = 'id INTEGER, name VARCHAR(255), score FLOAT'
spark.read.schema(schema).csv('/path/to/file.csv')
```
options.explanation: The DDL string uses SQL type names that do not map cleanly to Spark types — `VARCHAR(255)` is not a valid Spark DDL type (use `STRING`), and `FLOAT` maps to a 32-bit float rather than the required 64-bit `DOUBLE`. Spark's DDL string format requires Spark-specific type names: `INT` or `INTEGER`, `STRING`, `DOUBLE`, `LONG`, `BOOLEAN`, etc.

options.text:
```python
spark.read.csv('/path/to/file.csv', schema={'id': 'int', 'name': 'str', 'score': 'float'})
```
options.explanation: `spark.read.csv()` does not accept a `schema` keyword argument directly. Schemas must be passed via the `.schema()` method on the DataFrameReader (e.g., `spark.read.schema(schema).csv(path)`).

options.text:
```python
schema = {'id': 'int', 'name': 'str', 'score': 'float'}
spark.read.schema(schema).csv('/path/to/file.csv')
```
options.explanation: `spark.read.schema()` does not accept a Python dictionary. Schemas must be defined as a `StructType` object or a DDL-format string — Python dicts are not a valid schema format.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with a `timestamp` column of type TimestampType. Which code extracts the hour of day as an integer column?

options.text: `df.withColumn('hr', F.hour(col('timestamp')))`
options.explanation: `F.hour(col('timestamp'))` is the direct, idiomatic function for extracting the hour component from a TimestampType column. It returns an IntegerType column with values 0–23.

options.text: `df.withColumn('hr', F.date_format(col('timestamp'), 'HH').cast('int'))`
options.explanation: While `F.date_format(col('timestamp'), 'HH').cast('int')` works (formats as a zero-padded 2-digit string then casts), it is indirect and requires an extra cast step. `F.hour()` is the purpose-built function and is more efficient.

options.text: `df.withColumn('hr', col('timestamp').hour)`
options.explanation: PySpark Column objects do not expose an `.hour` attribute. Python attribute-style access (like `col.hour`) is a Pandas feature — in PySpark you must use `F.hour()` or `F.date_format()`.

options.text: `df.withColumn('hr', col('timestamp').getField('hour'))`
options.explanation: `col('timestamp').getField('hour')` is for accessing a named field inside a StructType column. TimestampType is not a struct — it has no `hour` field accessible via `getField()`, and this would raise an AnalysisException.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame `transactions` with columns `user_id`, `amount`, and `txn_date`. You want to compute, for each transaction, the sum of `amount` for that user over the past 7 days (including the current row's date). Which window spec is correct?

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy(col('txn_date').cast('long')).rangeBetween(-7 * 86400, 0)
```
options.explanation: `rangeBetween` with a numeric ORDER BY column computes boundaries as value offsets from the current row's ORDER BY value. To get 'past 7 days', the column must be numeric (seconds/milliseconds since epoch). Casting a DateType to `long` gives days-since-epoch, but `rangeBetween(-7 * 86400, 0)` with a long-cast timestamp column (in seconds) correctly specifies 7 days back. For date casts, use -7. The key insight is that RANGE-based frames work on value differences, requiring numeric ordering.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('txn_date').rowsBetween(-7, 0)
```
options.explanation: `rowsBetween(-7, 0)` looks at the 7 preceding physical rows, not rows within 7 days — if a user has 2 transactions in 8 days, this is wrong.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('txn_date').rangeBetween(-7, 0)
```
options.explanation: `rangeBetween(-7, 0)` with a date string/DateType orderBy computes range as -7 to 0 days difference only if the column is a numeric type.

options.text:
```python
from pyspark.sql.window import Window
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('txn_date').rowsBetween(Window.unboundedPreceding, 7)
```
options.explanation: `rowsBetween(unboundedPreceding, 7)` looks from the beginning of the partition to 7 rows after the current row — this is not a 7-day lookback.

questions.stem: You want to save a DataFrame as a Hive table (registered in the metastore). Which code accomplishes this?

options.text: `df.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('/warehouse/table')`
options.explanation: Writing to a path saves Parquet files to storage but does not register a table in the metastore — it cannot be queried by table name via `spark.sql()` without a subsequent `CREATE TABLE` DDL.

options.text: `df.write.mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('my_database.my_table')`
options.explanation: `saveAsTable(tableName)` writes the DataFrame to the Spark/Hive metastore as a managed or external table (depending on configuration). The table name can include the database: `'my_database.my_table'`.

options.text: `df.write.mode('overwrite').format('table').save('my_database.my_table')`
options.explanation: `format('table')` is not a valid DataFrameWriter format; the correct method to save as a table is `saveAsTable()`.

options.text: `df.createOrReplaceTempView('my_table')` then `spark.sql("SAVE TABLE my_table")`
options.explanation: `createOrReplaceTempView()` creates a session-scoped temp view, not a persistent table, and `SAVE TABLE` is not valid Spark SQL syntax.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about `F.explode()` versus `F.explode_outer()` when applied to an ArrayType column. (Select TWO)

options.text: `F.explode()` drops rows where the array is null or empty; `F.explode_outer()` keeps such rows with a null value in the exploded column.
options.explanation: This is the key functional difference — `explode()` silently drops rows with null or empty arrays (similar to an INNER behavior), while `explode_outer()` retains those rows and produces a single row with null in the exploded column (OUTER/LEFT behavior).

options.text: Both `explode()` and `explode_outer()` preserve the original array column alongside the exploded column.
options.explanation: By default, `explode()` and `explode_outer()` in a `select()` call replace the array column with the exploded values; to keep both, you would use `select('*', explode('array_col').alias('element'))` with explicit columns.

options.text: `F.explode_outer()` is the ANSI SQL standard function; `F.explode()` is deprecated.
options.explanation: Neither function is deprecated; `explode_outer()` is the OUTER variant of `explode()` and was added in Spark 2.2, but `explode()` is still current and widely used.

options.text: If a row has an array `[1, 2, 3]`, both `explode()` and `explode_outer()` produce three output rows for that input row.
options.explanation: For a non-null, non-empty array, both functions produce identical output — one row per element. The difference only manifests for null/empty arrays.

questions.stem: You want to broadcast a hint to the Spark SQL engine when using `spark.sql()`. Which SQL hint syntax achieves this?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM large_table JOIN small_table ON large_table.id = small_table.id OPTIONS (broadcast='small_table')`
options.explanation: `OPTIONS (broadcast='...')` is not a valid SQL construct for query hints.

options.text: `SELECT * FROM large_table BROADCAST JOIN small_table ON large_table.id = small_table.id`
options.explanation: `BROADCAST JOIN` is not valid Spark SQL syntax; the hint must be inside a `/*+ ... */` comment block.

options.text: `SELECT * FROM large_table JOIN small_table HINT BROADCAST ON large_table.id = small_table.id`
options.explanation: `HINT BROADCAST` is not valid SQL syntax; hints use the `/*+ ... */` comment notation.

options.text: `SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(small_table) */ * FROM large_table JOIN small_table ON large_table.id = small_table.id`
options.explanation: Spark SQL supports query hints using `/*+ HINT_NAME(table) */` syntax placed after the SELECT keyword. `BROADCAST(small_table)` tells the optimizer to broadcast `small_table` in the join, converting it to a BroadcastHashJoin.

questions.stem: What does `df.printSchema()` output?

options.text: The first 20 rows of the DataFrame in tabular format.
options.explanation: `df.show()` displays the first 20 rows; `printSchema()` shows schema metadata only.

options.text: A tree-format display of the DataFrame's column names, data types, and nullable flags.
options.explanation: `printSchema()` prints the DataFrame schema as a tree showing column names, their data types (e.g., StringType, IntegerType, ArrayType), and whether each column is nullable. This is useful for verifying schema after reads or complex transformations.

options.text: The underlying SQL query that was used to create the DataFrame.
options.explanation: `df.explain()` shows the execution plan (which may include the SQL query internally); `printSchema()` shows only the schema.

options.text: Statistics (count, mean, stddev, min, max) for each column.
options.explanation: `df.describe()` computes summary statistics; `printSchema()` shows schema structure only.

questions.stem:
You need to join three DataFrames `a`, `b`, `c` on `id`. You call:

```python
result = a.join(b, 'id').join(c, 'id')
```

What happens to the `id` column in the final result?

options.text: The result has three `id` columns, one from each source DataFrame.
options.explanation: String-based joins deduplicate the join key column — three `id` columns would appear if you used Column expression joins (`a.id == b.id`, etc.) three times.

options.text: The result has two `id` columns — string-based join merges the first two into one, but the second join on Column expression duplicates it.
options.explanation: Both joins use string-based syntax, so both merges happen and there is one `id` column total.

options.text: The result has one `id` column because both joins use the string-based column name, which merges the key into a single column.
options.explanation: When using a string column name in `join()`, PySpark merges the join key from both sides into a single output column. Chaining `a.join(b, 'id').join(c, 'id')` applies string-based join twice, resulting in exactly one `id` column in the final output.

options.text: The query raises an AnalysisException because chaining joins with the same column name is not allowed.
options.explanation: Chaining joins on the same column name is perfectly valid in PySpark.

questions.stem: Which of the following SQL statements creates a view in the Spark metastore that **persists across SparkSessions** (not a temp view)?

options.text: `CREATE OR REPLACE TEMP VIEW persistent_view AS SELECT * FROM orders`
options.explanation: `TEMP VIEW` is session-scoped — it is dropped when the session ends and is not visible to other sessions.

options.text: `CREATE GLOBAL TEMP VIEW persistent_view AS SELECT * FROM orders`
options.explanation: `GLOBAL TEMP VIEW` is stored in the `global_temp` database and is visible to other sessions within the same Spark application but is dropped when the application stops — it does not truly persist across application restarts.

options.text: `CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW my_database.persistent_view AS SELECT * FROM orders`
options.explanation: `CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW` (without TEMP or GLOBAL TEMP) creates a persistent view in the Hive/Spark metastore under the specified database. This view survives session termination and is visible to all sessions that connect to the same metastore.

options.text: `df.createOrReplaceTempView('persistent_view')`
options.explanation: `createOrReplaceTempView()` creates a session-scoped temp view, which does not persist.

questions.stem: You want to write a SQL query using the Spark catalog to check if a table named `customer_data` exists in the `analytics` database before dropping it. Which approach is correct?

options.text: `spark.sql('DROP TABLE analytics.customer_data')`
options.explanation: `DROP TABLE` without `IF EXISTS` raises an AnalysisException if the table does not exist — this is not safe without a prior existence check.

options.text:
```python
tables = [t.name for t in spark.catalog.listTables('analytics')]
if 'customer_data' in tables: spark.sql('DROP TABLE analytics.customer_data')
```
options.explanation: Iterating `spark.catalog.listTables()` and checking a Python list is an overly complex approach — it requires loading all table metadata from the catalog just to perform a simple existence check before a drop.

options.text:
```python
if spark.catalog.tableExists('customer_data', 'analytics'):
    spark.sql('DROP TABLE analytics.customer_data')
```
options.explanation: While using `spark.catalog.tableExists('customer_data', 'analytics')` and then dropping is valid, it requires two round-trips (catalog lookup + DDL statement) where the single `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS` accomplishes the same goal more efficiently.

options.text: `spark.sql('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS analytics.customer_data')`
options.explanation: `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS analytics.customer_data` is the idiomatic SQL approach. The `IF EXISTS` clause instructs the engine to silently succeed even when the table does not exist — no AnalysisException is raised. This is the standard, concise SQL pattern.

questions.stem: You execute `spark.sql('DESCRIBE EXTENDED my_table')`. What additional information does `EXTENDED` provide over `DESCRIBE my_table`?

options.text: `EXTENDED` returns sample data from the table in addition to column definitions.
options.explanation: `DESCRIBE EXTENDED` does not return sample data; `SELECT * FROM table LIMIT n` would do that.

options.text: `EXTENDED` includes detailed metadata such as table type (MANAGED/EXTERNAL), location, storage format, partition columns, and table properties.
options.explanation: `DESCRIBE EXTENDED table_name` provides all the column information from plain `DESCRIBE`, plus a detailed metadata section including: table type (MANAGED or EXTERNAL), location path, file format (Parquet, ORC, Delta, etc.), partition columns, SerDe info, table owner, creation time, and table properties.

options.text: `EXTENDED` shows the execution plan for the most recent query against the table.
options.explanation: Execution plans are shown via `EXPLAIN`, not `DESCRIBE EXTENDED`.

options.text: There is no difference; `EXTENDED` is an alias for `DESCRIBE` in Spark SQL.
options.explanation: `EXTENDED` does provide significantly more information than plain `DESCRIBE`.

questions.stem: Select TWO valid ways to list all tables available in the current database in a Spark SQL context. (Select TWO)

options.text: `spark.sql('SHOW TABLES').show()`
options.explanation: `SHOW TABLES` is valid Spark SQL that returns a DataFrame listing all tables (and views) in the current database, which you can display with `.show()`.

options.text: `spark.catalog.listTables()` — returns a list of Table objects
options.explanation: `spark.catalog.listTables()` returns a Python list of `Table` named tuples containing name, database, description, tableType, and isTemporary fields.

options.text: `spark.sql('LIST TABLES').show()`
options.explanation: `LIST TABLES` is not valid Spark SQL syntax; the correct command is `SHOW TABLES`.

options.text: `spark.tables()` — returns all tables as a DataFrame
options.explanation: `spark.tables()` is not a valid SparkSession method; the catalog API uses `spark.catalog.listTables()`.

questions.stem: You register a Python UDF via `spark.udf.register('my_func', my_python_fn, IntegerType())`. How can you use this in a SQL query?

options.text: `spark.sql('SELECT my_func(salary) FROM employees')`
options.explanation: `spark.udf.register(name, fn, returnType)` registers the function in the Spark SQL function registry under the given name. Once registered, it is callable in `spark.sql()` queries just like a built-in SQL function — `SELECT my_func(salary) FROM employees` is valid.

options.text: `spark.sql('SELECT PYTHON_UDF(my_func, salary) FROM employees')`
options.explanation: `PYTHON_UDF(name, col)` is not valid Spark SQL syntax.

options.text: Registered Python UDFs cannot be used in `spark.sql()` — they can only be used with DataFrame API `col()` or `expr()`.
options.explanation: Registered UDFs are specifically designed to be accessible in SQL queries — that is the primary purpose of `spark.udf.register()` vs the `@udf` decorator.

options.text: `spark.sql('SELECT CALL my_func(salary) FROM employees')`
options.explanation: `CALL my_func(...)` is not the correct SQL syntax for invoking UDFs in Spark SQL.

questions.stem: You run a PySpark job that calls `df.collect()` on a large DataFrame and the driver crashes with `java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space`. What is the most appropriate fix?

options.text: Increase `spark.executor.memory` to give executors more heap space.
options.explanation: Executor memory is irrelevant here — the OOM is on the driver, not the executors.

options.text: Increase `spark.driver.memory` or redesign the logic to avoid collecting all data to the driver (e.g., use `df.write()` or process data in Spark instead of Python).
options.explanation: `collect()` brings ALL data to the driver process as a Python list. If the DataFrame is large, the driver's JVM heap overflows. The solution is either: (1) increase `spark.driver.memory` if you truly need all data locally, or (2) redesign to avoid `collect()` — write results to storage with `df.write()`, use `show()` for inspection, or process the data in Spark.

options.text: Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` to split the data into smaller chunks.
options.explanation: `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` affects shuffle behavior; it does not change how much data is returned to the driver by `collect()`.

options.text: Add `df.cache()` before `collect()` to reduce memory pressure.
options.explanation: Caching the DataFrame stores it distributed across executors — it does not reduce the amount of data brought to the driver when `collect()` is called.

questions.stem:
You want to count events by type using an accumulator for debugging. Consider the following code:

```python
error_count = spark.sparkContext.accumulator(0)

def process_row(row):
    if row['status'] == 'error':
        error_count.add(1)
    return row

df.rdd.foreach(process_row)
print(f'Errors: {error_count.value}')
```

Which statement is TRUE about this code?

options.text: The code will fail because accumulators can only be used with RDD `map()`, not `foreach()`.
options.explanation: Accumulators can be used in any RDD operation that runs on executors, including `foreach()`, `map()`, `mapPartitions()`, etc.

options.text: The code correctly counts error rows; the accumulator value is reliably accurate after a `foreach()` action.
options.explanation: While the pattern is valid, 'reliably accurate' overstates the guarantee — task retries can cause overcounting.

options.text: The accumulator value is always 0 after the job because `foreach()` runs in the driver, not executors.
options.explanation: `foreach()` is a distributed action that runs the function on each partition's rows on the executors, not on the driver.

options.text: The accumulator may overcount if tasks are retried, but the pattern is valid and the final value is correct for successful task attempts in non-speculative execution.
options.explanation: Accumulators are valid in `foreach()` and will count error rows. However, if Spark retries a failed task (due to executor failure or node loss), the accumulator gets incremented again for the same rows. Without speculative execution, and assuming no task failures, the count is accurate. With task retries, it may overcount. Spark provides an 'exactly-once' guarantee for accumulators updated inside actions for the purpose of reliably tracking things, but retries can inflate the count in practice.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about reading the physical plan output from `df.explain()` for a query that joins two DataFrames. (Select TWO)

options.text: If the plan contains `Exchange hashpartitioning`, it indicates a shuffle is taking place — data is being redistributed across partitions.
options.explanation: `Exchange hashpartitioning(cols, n)` in the physical plan explicitly marks where Spark performs a shuffle — data is repartitioned across `n` partitions by hashing on the specified columns. This is the shuffle operation that creates a stage boundary.

options.text: If the plan contains `BroadcastHashJoin`, it means the join was performed entirely on the driver by collecting both DataFrames.
options.explanation: `BroadcastHashJoin` means one side was broadcast to all EXECUTORS (not the driver) as a hash table; the join runs distributed.

options.text: If the plan contains `SortMergeJoin`, it means both sides of the join were sorted and merged in a shuffle-free operation.
options.explanation: `SortMergeJoin` DOES require a shuffle (Exchange) before sorting — it is not shuffle-free. The shuffle happens to co-locate matching keys; then each partition's data is sorted and merged locally.

options.text: If the plan contains `FileScan parquet` with a `PushedFilters` section, it indicates Spark pushed predicates down to the Parquet reader to skip reading unnecessary data.
options.explanation: `PushedFilters` in the `FileScan parquet` section shows which filters were pushed down to the Parquet columnar reader. These filters allow Parquet to skip row groups (and even pages) that cannot satisfy the predicate, reducing I/O significantly.

questions.stem: You notice that after enabling AQE (`spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`), a job that previously used `SortMergeJoin` now uses `BroadcastHashJoin` without any code changes. What AQE feature caused this change?

options.text: AQE's shuffle partition coalescing reduced the number of partitions below the broadcast threshold.
options.explanation: Coalescing shuffle partitions changes the partition count, not the join strategy selection.

options.text: AQE automatically enables the External Shuffle Service, which makes all joins use BroadcastHashJoin.
options.explanation: The External Shuffle Service handles shuffle data persistence for dynamic allocation; it does not influence join strategy.

options.text: AQE dynamically switches join strategies at runtime — if actual runtime statistics show one side is smaller than `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`, it converts the join to a BroadcastHashJoin.
options.explanation: One of AQE's three main features is dynamic join strategy switching. At compile time, Spark may not know the exact size of intermediate results, so it conservatively picks SortMergeJoin. At runtime, after the build side of the join is materialized (e.g., after a filter or aggregation), AQE measures its actual size. If it falls below `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold`, AQE dynamically replaces the SortMergeJoin with a BroadcastHashJoin — without requiring any code changes.

options.text: AQE detected a data skew and split the larger partition, making it eligible for broadcasting.
options.explanation: Skew join handling splits skewed partitions within a join, but it does not convert the join type to broadcast.

questions.stem: Select TWO TRUE statements about Spark's **Tungsten execution engine**. (Select TWO)

options.text: Tungsten uses whole-stage code generation to compile multiple operators into a single Java function, reducing virtual function call overhead and enabling CPU cache-friendly processing.
options.explanation: Whole-stage code generation (WSCG) is a Tungsten feature that fuses multiple pipeline operators (e.g., filter, project, hash join) into a single generated Java method. This avoids virtual dispatch overhead between operators and produces tight loops that fit in CPU instruction caches.

options.text: Tungsten stores data in off-heap memory using a binary row format (UnsafeRow) that avoids JVM object overhead and reduces garbage collection.
options.explanation: Tungsten's binary row format (`UnsafeRow`) stores data off-heap or in compact on-heap binary form without JVM object headers. This dramatically reduces GC pressure and memory usage compared to storing data as Java objects.

options.text: Tungsten replaces the JVM entirely with a custom runtime environment written in C++.
options.explanation: Tungsten is implemented within the JVM (using Java bytecode generation via Janino); it does not replace the JVM.

options.text: Tungsten is only active when the `spark.tungsten.enabled=true` configuration is set.
options.explanation: Tungsten's features (code generation and off-heap memory) are enabled by default in Spark SQL; there is no `spark.tungsten.enabled` property to manually enable them.

questions.stem: What is the maximum number of tasks that can run concurrently on a single executor with `spark.executor.cores=4`?

options.text: 1 — executors process one partition at a time.
options.explanation: Executors run multiple concurrent tasks — one per allocated core.

options.text: Unlimited — tasks are scheduled until memory runs out.
options.explanation: The number of concurrent tasks is bounded by the number of cores; memory pressure may reduce throughput but the task count is limited by cores.

options.text: 4 — one task per core, so up to 4 concurrent tasks per executor.
options.explanation: `spark.executor.cores` sets the number of CPU cores allocated to each executor. Each core can run one task at a time, so an executor with 4 cores can run up to 4 concurrent tasks.

options.text: 200 — determined by `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`.
options.explanation: `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` controls the total number of shuffle partitions (and thus the maximum total parallelism across all executors), not the per-executor concurrency.

questions.stem: You are running a long iterative ML training job using MLlib. After 50 iterations, the job slows down significantly even though data volume is constant. `df.explain(True)` shows the logical plan has thousands of lines. What is the root cause and fix?

options.text: The job ran out of shuffle partitions; increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`.
options.explanation: Lineage length, not shuffle partitions, is the cause of this progressive slowdown.

options.text: Each iteration appends transformations to the lineage DAG. After many iterations the plan becomes enormous, causing Catalyst optimization to be slow. Fix: call `df.checkpoint()` every N iterations to truncate the lineage.
options.explanation: Iterative algorithms that repeatedly transform a DataFrame build increasingly long lineage chains — each iteration adds transformation nodes to the logical plan. After many iterations, the plan can have millions of nodes, causing the Catalyst optimizer to spend enormous amounts of time analyzing and optimizing it. This manifests as progressive slowdown with each iteration. The solution is to periodically call `df.checkpoint()` (after setting `spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir()`), which materializes the DataFrame to stable storage and creates a new DataFrame with a fresh, empty lineage — breaking the lineage chain.

options.text: Python GC pressure is accumulating; restart the Python worker processes between iterations.
options.explanation: Python GC would cause random slowdowns, not progressive per-iteration degradation correlated with iteration count.

options.text: The executor memory is fragmented; use `StorageLevel.DISK_ONLY` to persist all intermediate DataFrames.
options.explanation: `DISK_ONLY` persistence addresses memory pressure, not lineage accumulation.

questions.stem: Select TWO TRUE statements about how Spark handles **fault tolerance** for in-progress jobs. (Select TWO)

options.text: If an executor fails mid-job, Spark can recompute the lost partitions by replaying the transformations from the original data source or from a cached/checkpointed ancestor.
options.explanation: Spark's lineage-based fault tolerance allows it to recompute lost partitions by replaying transformations from the last available point in the DAG (original source, cached ancestor, or checkpoint). This is the core fault tolerance mechanism for executors.

options.text: If the driver fails, Spark automatically restarts the driver and recovers the job from where it left off.
options.explanation: Spark does NOT automatically recover from driver failures — the driver is a single point of failure. Some cluster managers (YARN with `--supervise`, or Kubernetes) can restart the driver, but this requires additional configuration and typically restarts the job from scratch.

options.text: Cached (in-memory) data on a failed executor is lost, and Spark must recompute those partitions from the lineage.
options.explanation: `df.cache()` stores data in executor memory — if that executor crashes, the cached data is gone. Spark does NOT automatically replicate cached data (unless using storage levels with `_2` suffix like `MEMORY_AND_DISK_2`). Spark will recompute lost partitions from the lineage when they are needed again.

options.text: Spark's fault tolerance relies entirely on data replication — every partition is replicated across 3 executors.
options.explanation: Spark does NOT replicate every partition by default — it relies on lineage recomputation, not storage replication (unlike HDFS).

questions.stem: You want to union two DataFrames `df1` and `df2` that have the same schema but with columns in different orders. Which method correctly aligns columns by name regardless of order?

options.text: `df1.union(df2)`
options.explanation: `df1.union(df2)` aligns columns by POSITION — if column order differs, values get mapped to wrong columns (column 1 of df1 aligns with column 1 of df2 regardless of name).

options.text: `df1.unionByName(df2)`
options.explanation: `df1.unionByName(df2)` aligns columns by name rather than position. If `df1` has columns `(a, b, c)` and `df2` has columns `(c, a, b)`, `unionByName` correctly matches `a` with `a`, `b` with `b`, `c` with `c`.

options.text: `df1.merge(df2)`
options.explanation: `merge()` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method.

options.text: `df1.append(df2)`
options.explanation: `append()` is not a valid PySpark DataFrame method (it exists for Pandas DataFrames).

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with an `ArrayType(StringType())` column `words` and want to filter rows where the array contains the element `'spark'`. Which expression is correct?

options.text: `df.filter(F.array_contains(col('words'), 'spark'))`
options.explanation: `F.array_contains(col, value)` returns a boolean Column that is True if the array contains the specified value. This is the idiomatic PySpark way to check array membership.

options.text: `df.filter(col('words').contains('spark'))`
options.explanation: `col('words').contains('spark')` is the Column method for checking if a STRING column contains a substring — it does not work on ArrayType columns and would cause a type error or AnalysisException.

options.text: `df.filter(col('words').includes('spark'))`
options.explanation: `.includes()` is not a valid PySpark Column method.

options.text: `df.filter(F.exists(col('words'), lambda x: x == 'spark'))`
options.explanation: `F.exists(col, lambda)` is a valid higher-order function (Spark 3+) that checks if any element satisfies a condition, but using a simple equality check with `array_contains` is more concise and efficient for this use case. Note: **D** is valid syntax in Spark 3+ but **A** is the more conventional and widely-tested answer.

questions.stem:
You execute the following code and observe the results. What is printed?

```python
df = spark.range(5)  # id: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
df2 = df.select(F.monotonically_increasing_id().alias('mid'), col('id'))
df2.show()
```

And then:
```python
df3 = df.repartition(3)
df4 = df3.select(F.monotonically_increasing_id().alias('mid'), col('id'))
df4.orderBy('id').show()
```

options.text: Both `df2` and `df4` produce consecutive IDs 0-4 for the `mid` column.
options.explanation: Repartitioning causes gaps in IDs.

options.text: `df2` produces consecutive IDs 0-4 (one partition, so no gaps). `df4` produces non-consecutive IDs with gaps at partition boundaries because repartitioning splits data across 3 partitions.
options.explanation: `spark.range(5)` by default creates a DataFrame with a small number of partitions (often 1 for small ranges or based on parallelism). With 1 partition, `monotonically_increasing_id()` would produce 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — consecutive because there's only one partition. After `repartition(3)`, the data is split into 3 partitions. `monotonically_increasing_id()` encodes the partition ID in the upper 31 bits of the 64-bit ID — partition 0 might produce IDs 0, 1; partition 1 produces 8589934592, 8589934593; partition 2 produces 17179869184. These are NOT consecutive.

options.text: `df2` and `df4` produce the same IDs because `monotonically_increasing_id()` is deterministic across repartitions.
options.explanation: IDs depend on partition assignment, which changes with repartitioning.

options.text: Both produce random IDs because `monotonically_increasing_id()` is non-deterministic.
options.explanation: `monotonically_increasing_id()` is deterministic for a given partition layout.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about `df.sample()` in PySpark. (Select TWO)

options.text: `df.sample(fraction=0.1)` returns approximately 10% of rows, but the exact count may vary since each row is independently included with 10% probability.
options.explanation: `df.sample(fraction=0.1)` uses Bernoulli sampling where each row is independently included with probability equal to `fraction`. The result is approximately 10% of rows, but the exact count is random — it follows a binomial distribution.

options.text: Providing a `seed` value to `df.sample()` guarantees that the same rows are returned every time the action is triggered, regardless of the number of partitions.
options.explanation: With the same seed and the same number of partitions, results are reproducible. But if partitioning changes (e.g., different `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`), the same seed may produce different rows because random number generation is per-partition.

options.text: `df.sample(withReplacement=True, fraction=2.0)` is valid and can produce a sample larger than the original DataFrame (bootstrap sampling).
options.explanation: When `withReplacement=True`, each row can be selected multiple times. A `fraction > 1.0` is valid with replacement and produces a bootstrap sample larger than the original — `fraction=2.0` produces approximately twice as many rows as the original.

options.text: `df.sample(fraction=0.1)` is equivalent to `df.limit(int(df.count() * 0.1))` — both produce exactly the same number of rows.
options.explanation: `limit(n)` takes the first n rows (deterministically from the beginning of the dataset), while `sample(fraction)` selects random rows from throughout the dataset — they are fundamentally different.

questions.stem: You want to write a user-defined aggregation function that computes a geometric mean. Which approach is most appropriate in PySpark?

options.text: Use a Python UDF with `@udf` decorator — pass each value one at a time.
options.explanation: Regular Python UDFs cannot be used as aggregation functions in `groupBy().agg()` — they operate row-by-row, not on grouped aggregates.

options.text: Use `F.pandas_udf` with `PandasUDFType.GROUPED_AGG` — receives all values for the group as a Pandas Series and returns a scalar.
options.explanation: For custom aggregation functions in PySpark, the `pandas_udf` with `PandasUDFType.GROUPED_AGG` (or the newer `@pandas_udf` returning a scalar, used in `groupBy().agg()`) is the recommended approach. It receives all values in a group as a Pandas Series, allowing you to apply any Python/NumPy computation. For geometric mean: `np.exp(np.log(s).mean())`. This runs in vectorized batches using Apache Arrow.

options.text: Use a regular Python UDF registered with `spark.udf.register()` as an aggregate function.
options.explanation: `spark.udf.register()` registers scalar UDFs for use in SQL; it cannot create aggregate UDFs directly.

options.text: Geometric mean cannot be implemented as a custom aggregation in PySpark.
options.explanation: Geometric mean is implementable via Pandas UDFs or by using `F.sum(F.log(col)) / F.count(col)` and exponentiating the result with built-in functions.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with columns `category` (StringType) and `amount` (DoubleType). You want to compute, for each row, how much `amount` is as a percentage of the total for that category. Which code is correct?

options.text:
```python
df.groupBy('category').agg(
    (col('amount') / F.sum('amount')).alias('pct')
)
```
options.explanation: `groupBy().agg()` collapses all rows in each group into a single summary row. Referencing `col('amount')` (a non-aggregate) inside an aggregation is invalid — Spark raises an AnalysisException because the column is not part of the GROUP BY.

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('category')
df.withColumn('pct', col('amount') / F.sum('amount').over(w) * 100)
```
options.explanation: A window function is the right tool for computing each row's percentage of its group total while KEEPING all original rows. `F.sum('amount').over(Window.partitionBy('category'))` computes the category total without collapsing rows, so the division `col('amount') / sum_total` computes each row's individual percentage inline.

options.text:
```python
category_totals = df.groupBy('category').agg(F.sum('amount').alias('total'))
df.join(category_totals, 'category')\
  .withColumn('pct', col('amount') / col('total') * 100)
```
options.explanation: While the join approach (compute totals, then join back) produces the correct result, it requires a shuffle for the groupBy AND another shuffle for the join — far less efficient than the window function in B.

options.text:
```python
df.groupBy('category')\
  .agg((F.sum('amount') / F.count('amount')).alias('pct'))
```
options.explanation: This code divides the sum of all amounts by the count of amounts, which is simply the average amount per category — not a per-row percentage of the total.

questions.stem: What does `F.date_trunc('year', col('event_ts'))` return for the timestamp `2024-11-20 15:45:30`?

options.text: `2024` — the year as an integer.
options.explanation: `F.year(col)` returns the year as an integer; `date_trunc` returns a full Timestamp value.

options.text: `2024-01-01 00:00:00` — the timestamp truncated to the start of the year.
options.explanation: `F.date_trunc('year', timestamp)` truncates the timestamp to the first moment of the year — `2024-01-01 00:00:00`. This is useful for grouping data by year or computing year-to-date aggregations. The return type is TimestampType.

options.text: `2024-11-20` — the date without the time component.
options.explanation: Truncating to 'day' gives `2024-11-20 00:00:00`; truncating to 'year' goes further back to January 1st.

options.text: `2024-12-31 23:59:59` — the last moment of the year.
options.explanation: `date_trunc` always truncates DOWN to the START of the period, never to the end.

questions.stem: You need to implement a pivot table showing total sales per product per quarter. The `txn_date` column is DateType and you don't know all possible quarters in advance. Which code correctly handles this?

options.text:
```python
df.withColumn('quarter', F.quarter(col('txn_date')))\
  .groupBy('product')\
  .pivot('quarter')\
  .agg(F.sum('amount'))
```
options.explanation: Using `F.quarter()` alone loses year information — all Q1s across different years would be merged into one column.

options.text:
```python
df.groupBy('product').pivot('txn_date').agg(F.sum('amount'))
```
options.explanation: Pivoting on raw dates creates one column per unique date, not per quarter.

options.text:
```python
df.groupBy('product', F.quarter(col('txn_date')).alias('qtr'))\
  .agg(F.sum('amount'))
```
options.explanation: `groupBy().agg()` without pivot collapses rows into a tabular format, not a pivot table with one column per quarter.

options.text:
```python
df.withColumn('quarter', F.concat(F.year(col('txn_date')).cast('string'),
              F.lit('Q'), F.quarter(col('txn_date')).cast('string')))\
  .groupBy('product').pivot('quarter').agg(F.sum('amount'))
```
options.explanation: To create a pivot table by quarter with both year and quarter information (e.g., '2024Q1', '2024Q2'), you need to create a composite quarter identifier. Simply using `F.quarter()` (answer A) would merge Q1 2023 with Q1 2024 into the same column. D creates a `quarter` string like '2024Q1' that uniquely identifies each year-quarter combination, then pivots on it.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct statements about `F.struct()` in PySpark. (Select TWO)

options.text: `F.struct('col1', 'col2')` creates a new StructType column containing `col1` and `col2` as nested fields.
options.explanation: `F.struct(*cols)` creates a new StructType column that bundles the specified columns as nested fields. The resulting column has type `StructType([StructField('col1', ...), StructField('col2', ...)])`.

options.text: The result of `F.struct()` can be serialized to JSON using `F.to_json(F.struct(...))`.
options.explanation: `F.to_json(F.struct(...))` is the standard pattern for converting multiple columns into a JSON string — first creating a struct, then serializing it. This is used for creating JSON payloads from DataFrame rows.

options.text: `F.struct()` requires all columns to have the same data type.
options.explanation: `F.struct()` can combine columns of different data types — structs in Spark can contain mixed-type fields (e.g., string, integer, array all in the same struct).

options.text: To access field `name` from a struct column `person`, you write `col('person').name` using Python attribute access.
options.explanation: PySpark Column objects do not support Python attribute access for struct fields (like Pandas does with `df.column`). To access a nested field, use `col('person.name')` or `col('person').getField('name')`.

questions.stem: Which of the following reads an ORC file and applies a filter pushdown for `department = 'Engineering'`?

options.text: `spark.read.orc('/path/data.orc').filter(col('department') == 'Engineering')`
options.explanation: Simply reading the ORC file and applying a `filter()` is sufficient — Catalyst's predicate pushdown optimization automatically pushes the filter into the ORC reader when the query is executed, so the ORC reader skips row groups that don't satisfy `department = 'Engineering'`. You don't need to explicitly configure pushdown.

options.text: `spark.read.format('orc').option('pushdown', 'department=Engineering').load('/path/data.orc')`
options.explanation: There is no `pushdown` option in the ORC reader API — pushdown is automatic.

options.text: `spark.read.orc('/path/data.orc', filterPredicate='department=Engineering')`
options.explanation: `spark.read.orc()` does not accept a `filterPredicate` keyword argument.

options.text: `spark.read.orc('/path/data.orc').where("department == 'Engineering'")`
options.explanation: is also a valid equivalent (`.where()` is an alias for `.filter()`), but **A** is listed as the answer because it uses the more commonly referenced syntax.

questions.stem: You have a DataFrame with `user_id` and `event` columns, and you want to compute, for each user, the number of events that occurred in the 3 rows BEFORE and AFTER each row (a 7-row rolling window). Which window specification is correct?

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time').rowsBetween(-3, 3)
```
options.explanation: `rowsBetween(-3, 3)` with an `orderBy` specifies a frame that includes 3 physical rows BEFORE the current row (offset -3) and 3 physical rows AFTER the current row (offset +3), for a total window of up to 7 rows. This is a symmetric rolling window centered on the current row.

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time').rangeBetween(-3, 3)
```
options.explanation: `rangeBetween(-3, 3)` computes boundaries based on value differences in the ORDER BY column — for a timestamp-based ordering, -3 means 3 units (seconds, milliseconds, etc.) before the current value, not 3 rows.

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').rowsBetween(-3, 3)
```
options.explanation: `rowsBetween()` requires `orderBy` to define row ordering — without `orderBy`, the row order is undefined.

options.text:
```python
w = Window.partitionBy('user_id').orderBy('event_time').rowsBetween(3, -3)
```
options.explanation: `rowsBetween(3, -3)` specifies the start > end, which is invalid (start must be <= end) and raises an AnalysisException.

questions.stem: You want to create an external Delta table named `raw_events` in the `landing` database pointing to data at `s3://bucket/raw/events/`. Which SQL statement is correct?

options.text: `CREATE TABLE landing.raw_events USING DELTA LOCATION 's3://bucket/raw/events/'`
options.explanation: `CREATE TABLE db.table USING format LOCATION 'path'` is the standard Spark SQL DDL for creating an external table. When `LOCATION` is specified, Spark creates an EXTERNAL table — the metastore registers the table definition but does not manage the data. `USING DELTA` specifies the storage format.

options.text: `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE landing.raw_events FORMAT DELTA AT 's3://bucket/raw/events/'`
options.explanation: `CREATE EXTERNAL TABLE ... FORMAT DELTA AT 'path'` is not valid Spark SQL syntax; `FORMAT` and `AT` are not the correct keywords.

options.text: `CREATE TABLE landing.raw_events DELTA EXTERNAL 's3://bucket/raw/events/'`
options.explanation: `DELTA EXTERNAL 'path'` is not valid syntax — the correct clauses are `USING DELTA LOCATION`.

options.text: `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE landing.raw_events USING DELTA OPTIONS (path 's3://bucket/raw/events/')`
options.explanation: `OPTIONS (path '...')` is used in some contexts but for external table registration the explicit `LOCATION` clause is the standard and most reliable syntax.

questions.stem: Select TWO correct use cases for `CACHE TABLE` in Spark SQL. (Select TWO)

options.text: Caching a small dimension table that is joined multiple times in the same session, to avoid re-reading it from storage for each join.
options.explanation: `CACHE TABLE dim_products` is ideal for frequently-joined dimension tables. Once cached, Spark reads the data from in-memory storage instead of re-scanning files on every join. This is especially effective for star-schema queries.

options.text: Caching a table to persist its data across SparkSession restarts, enabling faster cold starts.
options.explanation: SQL `CACHE TABLE` is session-scoped and in-memory only — it does not persist across session restarts. Persistent storage requires writing to Delta/Parquet.

options.text: Caching the result of an expensive aggregation stored as a temp view, so repeated queries against that view do not re-execute the aggregation.
options.explanation: You can cache a temp view that represents a complex aggregation (`CREATE OR REPLACE TEMP VIEW summary AS SELECT ...`, then `CACHE TABLE summary`). Subsequent queries against `summary` skip the re-aggregation and read from cache.

options.text: Caching a table to reduce the size of its Parquet files by compressing them into a single cached file.
options.explanation: `CACHE TABLE` stores data in Spark's in-memory store; it does not write or compact Parquet files on disk.

questions.stem: You have a Delta table `orders` partitioned by `order_date`. You run `MSCK REPAIR TABLE orders`. What happens?

options.text: Spark scans the table's directory for Hive-style partition directories and adds any new partitions to the metastore.
options.explanation: This describes the behavior for Hive-style tables, not Delta tables.

options.text: `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` is the correct command to vacuum old versions of the Delta table.
options.explanation: `VACUUM table_name` removes old Delta table versions; `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` does not perform vacuuming.

options.text: For a Delta table, `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` has no effect — Delta's transaction log already tracks all partition information. You should use `REFRESH TABLE orders` instead for Delta tables.
options.explanation: `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` is designed for Hive-style partitioned tables where partition directories follow the `key=value` naming convention. Delta tables do NOT use Hive-style partition discovery — all partition information is tracked in the Delta transaction log (`_delta_log/`). Running `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` on a Delta table is a no-op at best. For Delta tables, `REFRESH TABLE table_name` updates the catalog cache, or simply querying the table is sufficient since the Delta log is always authoritative.

options.text: `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` computes statistics on the Delta table's partition columns.
options.explanation: `ANALYZE TABLE table_name COMPUTE STATISTICS` computes statistics; `MSCK REPAIR TABLE` does not.

questions.stem: You want to add a column with a default value when running `ALTER TABLE`. Which SQL statement adds a `status STRING` column with a default value of `'active'` to a Delta table?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (status STRING DEFAULT 'active')`
options.explanation: In Spark SQL (especially for Delta tables on Databricks), `ALTER TABLE table ADD COLUMNS (col_name TYPE DEFAULT value)` is the syntax for adding a column with a default value. For Delta tables, this default value is stored in the table metadata and used for existing rows when queried.

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (status STRING) SET DEFAULT 'active'`
options.explanation: `SET DEFAULT` is not valid SQL syntax for column default values in Spark SQL.

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN status STRING = 'active'`
options.explanation: `ADD COLUMN col = value` using `=` is not valid Spark SQL DDL syntax for specifying defaults.

options.text: `ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (status STRING); UPDATE orders SET status = 'active' WHERE status IS NULL`
options.explanation: While functionally equivalent (add column then fill nulls with UPDATE), it is not the SQL ALTER TABLE syntax for defaults — and for large tables, the UPDATE would be a very expensive full-table operation.

questions.stem: You run `ANALYZE TABLE sales COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS amount, category`. What does this do and when is it useful?

options.text: It computes the total count and average of `amount` and `category` columns and prints them to the console.
options.explanation: The statistics are stored in the metastore for the optimizer to use, not printed to the console.

options.text: It collects column-level statistics (min, max, distinct count, null count, histogram) and stores them in the metastore for use by Spark's cost-based optimizer to make better join strategy decisions.
options.explanation: `ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS` collects detailed column statistics — min value, max value, approximate number of distinct values (NDV), number of nulls, and histogram buckets — and stores them in the Hive metastore. These statistics are used by Spark SQL's cost-based optimizer (CBO) to make better decisions about join order, join strategy (e.g., which side to broadcast), and aggregation strategies. This is particularly important for complex multi-table joins.

options.text: It creates an index on the `amount` and `category` columns to speed up filter queries.
options.explanation: Spark SQL does not support creating column indexes in the traditional database sense; predicate pushdown handles filter optimization at the file level.

options.text: It triggers Spark to cache the `amount` and `category` columns in memory.
options.explanation: Caching is triggered by `CACHE TABLE`, not `ANALYZE TABLE`.

questions.stem: You run `df.explain('formatted')` (Spark 3.0+ syntax). How does this differ from `df.explain(True)`?

options.text: `explain('formatted')` outputs only the physical plan; `explain(True)` outputs all four plan types.
options.explanation: `explain('formatted')` shows all plan phases, not just the physical plan.

options.text: `explain('formatted')` is a deprecated alias for `explain(False)` in Spark 3.0+.
options.explanation: `explain('formatted')` is a new addition in Spark 3.0, not deprecated.

options.text: `explain('formatted')` outputs all plans in a structured, more readable format with node IDs and metrics; `explain(True)` outputs the same information but in a less structured linear format.
options.explanation: Spark 3.0 introduced string-based `explain()` modes: `'simple'` (physical plan only), `'extended'` (all four plans, equivalent to `explain(True)`), `'codegen'` (generated Java code), `'cost'` (cost-based stats), and `'formatted'` (a structured output with clearly delineated sections, node IDs like `(1)`, and indented child nodes for better readability). `explain('formatted')` shows the same content as `explain(True)` but in a significantly more human-readable format.

options.text: `explain('formatted')` triggers execution and shows actual runtime metrics; `explain(True)` shows only static plan information.
options.explanation: No variant of `explain()` triggers execution or shows runtime metrics — that information is only available in the Spark UI after job completion.

questions.stem: You set `spark.sparkContext.setCheckpointDir('hdfs://namenode/checkpoints')` and then call `df.checkpoint()`. What are the characteristics of the checkpointed data?

options.text: The data is written to the driver's local disk at the specified HDFS path.
options.explanation: The data is written to the DISTRIBUTED storage path (HDFS), not the driver's local disk.

options.text: The data is written to HDFS in Parquet format, the lineage is truncated, and the resulting DataFrame reads from the checkpoint directory.
options.explanation: Checkpoint format is Spark's internal binary format, not Parquet.

options.text: The data is written to HDFS in Spark's internal binary format; the new DataFrame has no lineage (empty plan), and if the checkpoint data is deleted, the DataFrame cannot be recomputed.
options.explanation: `df.checkpoint()` (reliable checkpoint) writes the data to the HDFS/S3 checkpoint directory in Spark's internal binary row format (not Parquet). The critical characteristic is that it TRUNCATES the lineage — the returned DataFrame has no logical plan history. This means: (1) if the checkpoint files are deleted, the data is gone and cannot be recomputed; (2) subsequent `explain()` on the checkpointed DataFrame shows a simple `LocalCheckpoint` or `FileScan` at the root with no upstream dependencies.

options.text: The data is written lazily — the checkpoint only executes when the next action is triggered.
options.explanation: `df.checkpoint()` is EAGER — it triggers computation immediately (unlike `cache()` which is lazy). It blocks until data is written to the checkpoint directory.

questions.stem: Select TWO best practices for writing testable PySpark code. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write transformation logic as pure functions that take a DataFrame as input and return a DataFrame, making them easy to unit test with a local SparkSession.
options.explanation: Designing transformation functions as pure functions (input DataFrame → output DataFrame) is the key to unit testability in PySpark. You can create a test SparkSession, build small input DataFrames with known data, pass them through the function, and assert on the results. This promotes separation of concerns and makes tests deterministic.

options.text: Use `df.collect()` extensively in your transformation functions to convert intermediate results to Python lists for easier processing.
options.explanation: Using `collect()` inside transformation functions defeats the purpose of distributed processing — transformations should stay distributed. `collect()` should only be used at the final assertion step in tests, not within the transformation logic itself.

options.text: Avoid using `spark.sql()` for core transformation logic — prefer the DataFrame API which can be more easily mocked and verified in unit tests.
options.explanation: `spark.sql()` can be perfectly unit tested with a local SparkSession and temp views; avoiding it entirely is unnecessarily restrictive.

options.text: Always use `StructType` schemas to define expected output schemas explicitly, making it easy to assert that transformations produce the correct schema.
options.explanation: Explicitly defining schemas with `StructType` allows you to assert that the output DataFrame has exactly the expected columns, types, and nullability. Schema-level assertions catch many bugs early and are much faster than checking actual data values.

questions.stem: You see `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER_2` as a StorageLevel in a persist call. What do `_SER` and `_2` indicate?

options.text: `_SER` means data is stored serialized (using Java/Kryo serialization) rather than as deserialized Java objects. `_2` means data is replicated to 2 executors for fault tolerance.
options.explanation: The `_SER` suffix in a StorageLevel name (e.g., `MEMORY_ONLY_SER`, `MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER`) indicates that partitions are stored in SERIALIZED form — as compact byte arrays using Java serialization (or Kryo if configured). This uses less memory than storing deserialized objects (no JVM object headers, fewer pointers) but requires deserialization on each access. The `_2` suffix means each partition is replicated to 2 executor nodes, providing fault tolerance — if one executor fails, the data is still available on the second. This increases memory/disk usage 2x but avoids recomputation on failure.

options.text: `_SER` means data is compressed with Snappy. `_2` means data is written to 2 disk locations.
options.explanation: `_SER` is serialization format, not compression codec (compression is separate). `_2` is replication factor, not disk locations.

options.text: `_SER` means data is encrypted. `_2` means data is split across 2 partitions per executor.
options.explanation: Spark's StorageLevel does not have built-in encryption; `_SER` is serialization.

options.text: `_SER` and `_2` are internal Spark optimization flags that users do not need to configure.
options.explanation: `_SER` and `_2` are meaningful configuration choices that significantly affect performance and fault tolerance.

questions.stem: You use `df.localCheckpoint()` in an iterative algorithm instead of `df.checkpoint()`. What is the key risk?

options.text: `localCheckpoint()` does not truncate the lineage, so it does not solve the long-lineage problem.
options.explanation: `localCheckpoint()` DOES truncate the lineage — that is one of the reasons to use it.

options.text: `localCheckpoint()` is always slower than `checkpoint()` because local disk I/O is slower than HDFS.
options.explanation: Local disk I/O is typically FASTER than HDFS (no network transfer), which is why `localCheckpoint()` is faster than reliable `checkpoint()`.

options.text: `localCheckpoint()` stores data on executor local disk. If any executor fails, the checkpointed partition data on that executor is lost and CANNOT be recomputed (lineage is truncated), potentially causing the job to fail.
